dockerfile.value:
FROM ubuntu:24.04

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
ENV BUN_INSTALL=/root/.bun
ENV PATH="/root/.fabro/bin:/root/.bun/bin:/root/.local/bin:/root/.qlty/bin:${PATH}"
WORKDIR /home/daytona

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends bash ca-certificates curl git jq openssh-client python3 python3-pip pipx ripgrep shellcheck unzip xz-utils && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_22.x | bash - && apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends nodejs && npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.141 @openai/codex promptfoo && npm cache clean --force && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

RUN curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash -s "bun-v1.2.22" && ln -sf /root/.bun/bin/bun /usr/local/bin/bun

RUN pipx install spec-kitty-cli==3.1.8 && curl -fsSL https://qlty.sh | sh && ln -sf /root/.qlty/bin/qlty /usr/local/bin/qlty

RUN set -eux; target="x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu"; asset="fabro-${target}.tar.gz"; url="$(curl -fsSL https://api.github.com/repos/fabro-sh/fabro/releases/latest | jq -r --arg asset "$asset" '.assets[] | select(.name == $asset) | .browser_download_url')"; test -n "$url"; curl -fsSL "$url" -o /tmp/fabro.tar.gz; tar -xzf /tmp/fabro.tar.gz -C /tmp; install -m 0755 "/tmp/fabro-${target}/fabro" /usr/local/bin/fabro; rm -rf /tmp/fabro.tar.gz "/tmp/fabro-${target}"; fabro --version

CMD ["/bin/bash"]

Be explicit about evidence quality. Do not overfit to the largest apps if smaller fast-growing products better match the opportunity.

# Product Fidelity Review

Review the generated app against `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/spec.md` and research synthesis.

Write `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/reviews/product-fidelity.md`.

Reject if the app is generic, template-like, or misses the core researched user pain.

# Independent Architecture Candidate

Read:

- `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/spec.md`
- `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/definition-of-done.md`
- `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/quality-bar.json`
- SwiftAIBoilerplatePro docs if available in the app repo or GitHub.

Create an architecture plan that extends SwiftAIBoilerplatePro instead of rebuilding it.

Cover:

- modules/packages to keep unchanged
- modules/packages to adapt
- modules/packages to remove only if justified
- CompositionRoot changes
- DesignSystem usage
- data/repository/client plan
- ViewModel boundaries
- Swift 6 concurrency risks
- test plan
- Appium exploratory harness plan
- GitHub macOS CI plan

Write the architecture candidate in your response with a clear model identity.

# Verify Current Phase

Read the latest phase evidence in `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/evidence/`.

independently verify:

- the phase did not exceed its scope
- the evidence lists files, commands, acceptance criteria, and risks
- boilerplate conventions were followed
- no obvious quality gate was weakened or deferred without stating why
- any generated code remains testable and modular

If you find issues, explain the exact retry target and what to fix. If acceptable, update the same evidence file with a short `Verifier notes` section.

# AI UI Explorer

Run or complete the automated exploratory UI pass for `apps/waketask-iphone`.

The target behavior is: launch the iOS app in simulator through Appium/XCUITest and tap every reachable enabled button/control across the MVP flows.

The explorer should:

- record each screen source or screenshot
- identify buttons, tabs, cells, navigation controls, and primary actions
- tap controls breadth-first with a bounded depth
- recover with back/navigation when possible
- detect crashes, blank screens, stuck spinners, and unhandled error screens
- produce `reports/ios/appium-exploratory-report.json`

The report JSON must include:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "buttons_tapped": 0,
  "screens_visited": 0,
  "crashes": 0,
  "failures": 0,
  "failures_detail": []
}
```

Do not mark the pass complete unless a real simulator/Appium run happened, unless the workflow explicitly allows macOS deferral.

# Implement Integration Phase

Implement only the integration and release-hardening phase in `apps/waketask-iphone`.

Scope:

- wire feature into CompositionRoot/AppShell
- complete tests and UI test fixtures
- complete `scripts/ci/ios-quality.sh`
- complete `.github/workflows/ios-quality.yml`
- complete Appium exploratory script
- generate `reports/ios/ios-quality-report.json` when quality checks pass
- generate `reports/ios/appium-exploratory-report.json` when Appium checks pass
- create release/readiness docs

The CI script must include xcodebuild build/test, SwiftLint, SwiftFormat, Qlty, secret scan, and App Store string audit.

Secret handling is blocking: never print environment variables or credential values. CI and local scripts may verify that required secrets are present, but output must be redacted true/false status only.

Write `.workflow/iphone-app-factory/evidence/integration.md` with:

- `Risks`

# Implement Core Phase

Implement only the core product logic phase in `apps/waketask-iphone`.

Use the boilerplate's existing module boundaries, repositories, clients, logging, and Swift concurrency patterns.

Core scope:

- domain models
- repositories/clients or local persistence needed for MVP
- ViewModel logic without UI layout work
- unit tests for logic, repositories, and ViewModels
- privacy-preserving data handling

Do not add broad infrastructure that the boilerplate already provides.

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_5","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,260p' /home/daytona/workspace/.workflow/iphone-app-factory/research-synthesis.md"","aggregated_output":"# Research Synthesis: WakeTask — Task-Driven Wake-Up Alarm & Accountability

## Executive Summary

WakeTask is a task-driven alarm app positioned between Apple Clock's simplicity and Alarmy's feature complexity. It forces morning task completion through deliberate interaction, optional lightweight accountability, and calendar-aware scheduling—addressing a 120-150K user gap of people who want their actual morning tasks at alarm time, not generic puzzles.

---

## Target User & Urgent Problem

### Primary User: The Frustrated Morning Task Person
- **Demographics**: 25-45, employed or studying, smartphone-dependent
- **Context**: Works from home or has flexible start time; lost natural wake trigger
- **Behavior**: Uses task manager (Todoist/Reminders) but abandons morning routine; hits snooze 3-4x daily
- **Device Habits**: Phone within arm's reach at night; checks calendar and tasks first thing

### Secondary Users (Segment Growth Opportunities)
1. **Remote Workers** (9 mentions, Reddit): Lost commute friction; need artificial structure
2. **Neurodivergent/ADHD Users** (22 mentions, Reddit): Willpower insufficient; need external forcing
3. **Parents of Young Children** (9 mentions, Reddit): Pre-existing chaos; need 60-sec morning clarity before kids wake
4. **Students/Flexible Schedule** (implied): Alarm time varies by day; need calendar-aware flexibility

### Urgent Problem: The 30-Second Wake Trap
Users wake up with **30-90 seconds of attention budget** before sleep inertia recaptures them. In that window:
- Apple Clock option: Just dismiss alarm (no task context)
- Alarmy option: Solve math puzzle (generic, disconnected from actual morning)
- Current workflow: Check phone, find task list, feel overwhelmed, snooze
- **Gap**: No app connects alarm moment to actual morning task

**Evidence**:
- "I need my morning task to appear at alarm time, not hunt for it" (34 App Store + Reddit mentions)
- "Friction works but then I go back to bed anyway" (24 mentions)
- "Works great until I get used to it" (21 mentions)

---

## Jobs-to-be-Done

### Primary Job: Get Out of Bed Reliably
**Context**: Heavy sleeper who sets multiple alarms; snoozes regularly
**Job Statement**: When I wake up, I need to get out of bed quickly so I can start my day on time and not feel rushed
**Current Solution**: Setting 10 alarms (ineffective); depends on partner (not scalable)
**Success Metric**: Rise on first or second alarm (<2 snoozes) 4+ days/week

### Secondary Job: Prioritize Morning Ruthlessly
**Context**: Overwhelmed by full task list at wake time
**Job Statement**: When I wake up, I need to know my top 1-2 priorities instantly so I don't waste time deciding
**Current Solution**: Bullet journal night before; sticky note on mirror
**Success Metric**: Identify priorities in <30 seconds; complete top task in first 30 min

### Tertiary Job: Build Sustainable Morning Habit
**Context**: Apps work 2-3 weeks then novelty wears off
**Job Statement**: When I want to maintain a morning routine, I need something that stays effective long-term so I don't revert to old patterns
**Current Solution**: Financial stakes (Nuj, Beeminder) or accountability partner (text daily)
**Success Metric**: 80%+ of mornings completing first task by week 4+

### Quaternary Job: Feel Supported, Not Shamed
**Context**: Struggles with willpower; tried social accountability but felt exposed
**Job Statement**: When I'm struggling to wake up, I need accountability that helps without making me feel bad so I can sustain the habit
**Current Solution**: Tell partner privately; avoid public social posting
**Success Metric**: Optional check-in with trusted person (not public, not mandatory)

---

## Top Complaint Themes & Quoted Evidence Summaries

### 1. Feature Bloat Dilutes Core (31 mentions)
**Evidence**: "Alarmy has games/music I don't want; just give me the task forcing"
**Root Cause**: Designers optimize for engagement/retention; users want single purpose
**Implication**: WakeTask must ruthlessly exclude non-essential features (no music, no games, no social posting)

### 2. Friction Alone Insufficient (24 mentions)
**Evidence**: "Moved phone/scanned barcode but then just went back to bed"
**Root Cause**: Friction without purpose fails; brain learns to tolerate friction
**Implication**: Task forcing must be **purposeful friction** (actual morning goal, not arbitrary)

### 3. Retention Drop After 2-3 Weeks (21 mentions)
**Evidence**: "Works great until I get used to it"
**Root Cause**: Psychological novelty wears off; no refresh mechanism
**Implication**: Must support optional stakes/accountability that doesn't habituate (vs. gamified rewards)

### 4. Social Features Feel Extreme (18 mentions)
**Evidence**: "Don't want to post to social media, but app only offers that"
**Root Cause**: I Can't Wake Up's model too exposed; users want micro-accountability instead
**Implication**: Accountability must be optional, lightweight, and private (not public posts)

### 5. Setup/Configuration Burden (15 mentions)
**Evidence**: "Apps require lots of setup before first alarm"
**Root Cause**: Power users want customization; casual users want zero-config
**Implication**: MVP must work immediately (no configuration); advanced options in settings

---

## Competitor Strengths & Weaknesses

### Alarmy: Strength in Task Forcing, Weakness in Purpose
**Strengths**:
- Proven task forcing works (245K reviews validates demand)
- Multiple task types (choice perception)
- Reliable alarm quality

**Weaknesses**:
- Generic tasks (math, puzzle, photo) disconnected from actual morning
- Expensive ($4.99/mo = $60/year)
- Feature bloat (games, music unused by users)
- No accountability; solo forcing only
- No integration with personal task systems

**WakeTask Advantage**: Personal tasks + optional accountability + free

### SnoozeProof: Strength in Simplicity, Weakness in Purpose
**Strengths**:
- Extremely simple; users adopt immediately
- Growing (validates minimalist demand)
- Friction-based forcing neuroscience-backed

**Weaknesses**:
- Friction alone insufficient; users return to bed
- No task integration
- No accountability
- Growth plateau

**WakeTask Advantage**: Friction + task purpose + accountability

### I Can't Wake Up: Strength in Accountability, Weakness in Scope
**Strengths**:
- Social accountability validated as highest-impact
- Real engagement signal

**Weaknesses**:
- Social posting too extreme for many users
- No task integration
- Habituation after 2 weeks
- No calendar integration

**WakeTask Advantage**: Optional micro-accountability + tasks + calendar

### Nuj: Strength in Stakes, Weakness in Access
**Strengths**:
- Financial stakes most effective motivator
- Real skin-in-game

**Weaknesses**:
- Trust barrier (payment in alarm app)
- No task integration
- Extreme for casual users
- Micro user base (1.2K reviews)

**WakeTask Advantage**: Optional lightweight stakes + free core + task integration

---

## Feature Opportunity Matrix

| Opportunity | Evidence | User Pain | MVP Feature | Differentiation | Risk |
|-------------|----------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| **Personal Task at Alarm** | 34 mentions | "Hunt for task list at wake" | Show task from Reminders/custom list at alarm dismiss | First app combining alarm + personal tasks | Low |
| **Lightweight Accountability** | 22 mentions | "Social posting too extreme" | Optional SMS/iMessage check-in to one person | Micro-accountability (not public) | Low |
| **Calendar-Aware Forcing** | 16 mentions | "Fixed alarm doesn't fit calendar" | Task priority rises if meeting imminent; optional early alarm | First app integrating calendar urgency into alarm | Medium |
| **Snooze-Proof Task Re-presentation** | 19 mentions | "Forget task after snooze" | Each snooze shows task again (not just time) | Task reminder on every snooze | Low |
| **Lightweight Stakes** | 18 mentions | "Financial commitment too extreme" | Optional 1-min/morning streak or micro-stake ($0.25) | Stakes optional and low-cost | Medium |
| **Health Integration** | 11 mentions | "Don't wake if slept <6h" | Skip alarm if sleep duration too low | Health-aware forcing | Medium |
| **Habit Persistence** | 15 mentions | "Tricks work 2-3 weeks" | Rotate task presentation (visual/haptic/audio) | Refresh mechanism vs. habituation | High |

---

## Differentiation Thesis for App Store 4.3 Review

### Core Thesis: Personal Tasks, Not Generic Games
WakeTask is the first alarm app that forces you to interact with **your actual morning task**, not a puzzle designed by the developer. By connecting your personal task list (Reminders, Todoist, custom) directly to your alarm, WakeTask ensures the only way to dismiss snooze is to commit to what matters to you that morning.

### Sub-Thesis #1: Accountability Without Shame
Optional lightweight check-in with a trusted person (not public posts, not financial stakes). You decide if accountability helps you; it's never forced or exposed.

### Sub-Thesis #2: Calendar-Aware & Context-Smart
If you have a 6am meeting tomorrow, WakeTask wakes you earlier. If your day is light, you can sleep longer. Calendar urgency shapes alarm behavior; alarm doesn't ignore your life.

### Sub-Thesis #3: Free, Simple, Focused
No subscription for core features. No games, music, or social posting. Just the alarm, the task, and the accountability you choose. Built for people who already use Reminders/task managers; WakeTask is the bridge to actually doing them.

### Why This Wins for App Store Review
1. **Clear Differentiation**: Personal tasks vs. competitors' generic games/friction
2. **Privacy-First**: On-device task storage; optional accountability; no tracking
3. **Accessibility-Inclusive**: VoiceOver + haptic forcing for all abilities
4. **No Dark Patterns**: Free core; no dark patterns; no manipulative gamification
5. **Aligned with iOS Values**: Deep Calendar/Reminders integration; Health awareness; system-level design

---

## Design Direction

### Alarm Screen UX
- **Large task text** (40+ pt font, high contrast)
- **One primary action**: "Complete Task" button (bright, unmissable)
- **One secondary action**: "Snooze 10 min" (smaller, gray)
- **Haptic feedback**: Strong, persistent pulse (Deaf accessibility)
- **Minimal cognitive load**: Binary choice (Complete or Snooze); groggy user friendly

### Task Selection (Setup)
- **On launch**: "Which app has your morning tasks?"
  - [ ] Apple Reminders (default)
  - [ ] Todoist
  - [ ] Custom list (WakeTask-managed)
- **No complex setup**: Pick app, grant permission, done
- **Dynamic task loading**: Each morning, pull top-priority task from chosen source

### Accountability Option (Setup)
- **Optional**: "Do you want a check-in person?"
  - [ ] No (solo mode)
  - [ ] Yes: Pick contact (SMS or iMessage weekly check-in)
- **Lightweight**: Single check-in message once per week, if enabled

### Lock Screen Widget (iOS 16+)
- **Shows**: Alarm time + task name (e.g., "6:30 • Write email to manager")
- **Tap action**: Launch WakeTask with task pre-selected

### Gesture Interaction
- **Swipe right**: Snooze 10 min (haptic feedback)
- **Tap large button**: Complete task (3x haptic pulse; task marked done in Reminders)
- **Force press** (if available): Quick task edit

---

## MVP Scope & Non-Goals

### MVP Scope (Week 1-4)
1. **Alarm Forcing**
   - Set alarm time + recurrence (weekday/custom)
   - Critical alert sound + haptic forcing
   - Persistent notification until task completed or snooze selected

2. **Task Integration**
   - Connect to Apple Reminders (read-only)
   - Pull top-priority task at alarm time
   - Display task text on alarm screen (40+ pt font)

3. **Snooze & Complete**
   - Snooze action (10, 15, 30 min options)
   - Complete action (marks task done in Reminders)
   - Each snooze re-shows task (not just time)

4. **Accessibility**
   - VoiceOver support
   - Haptic forcing for Deaf users
   - Dynamic Type support (text resizing)
   - High contrast dark mode

5. **Settings**
   - Alarm time + recurrence
   - Snooze duration
   - Task source (Reminders only, MVP)
   - Haptic intensity

### Post-MVP (Months 2-3)
- [ ] Calendar integration (urgency awareness)
- [ ] Lightweight accountability (SMS/iMessage check-in)
- [ ] Todoist integration
- [ ] Lock screen widget
- [ ] Siri Shortcuts (voice task entry)

## Risks & Unknowns

### Risk 1: Task List Switching (Medium)
**Risk**: Users may not grant Reminders permission or may use different task manager (Todoist, Things 3)
**Impact**: MVP limited to Apple Reminders only; excludes Todoist users (large segment)
**Mitigation**: 
- Post-MVP priority: Todoist integration (API available)
- Custom task entry fallback (WakeTask-managed tasks for MVP)
- Clear messaging: "Works best with Apple Reminders"

### Risk 2: Retention Habituation (High)
**Risk**: Task forcing may habituate after 2-3 weeks (same as Alarmy, I Can't Wake Up)
**Impact**: User churn post-trial; requires refresh mechanism to sustain
**Mitigation**:
- Post-MVP: Rotation of task presentation (visual/haptic/audio variety)
- Optional lightweight stakes (micro-streak, not gamification)
- Community features (optional peer challenges, post-MVP)

### Risk 3: Accountability Awkwardness (Medium)
**Risk**: Users may feel uncomfortable with check-in person; could cause relationship friction
**Impact**: Feature unused; no differentiation vs. competitors
**Mitigation**:
- Keep it lightweight (1x/week, optional opt-in)
- Clear messaging: "For support, not judgment"
- Easy opt-out at any time
- User testing with target segment before launch

### Risk 4: Health Integration Trust (Medium)
**Risk**: Users hesitant to share sleep data; privacy concerns
**Impact**: Feature adoption low; requires clear privacy policy
**Mitigation**:
- On-device only (no cloud sync for health data)
- Transparent privacy policy (public, detailed)
- Optional feature (works without Health integration)
- User testing on privacy concerns before launch

### Risk 5: Calendar Integration Complexity (Medium)
**Risk**: Calendar integration requires EventKit permissions + logic for meeting detection
**Impact**: Post-MVP feature; MVP does not have this
**Mitigation**:
- MVP: Simple fixed-time alarm (no calendar awareness)
- Post-MVP: Calendar integration after Reminders validation

### Risk 6: iOS Version Fragmentation (Low)
**Risk**: iOS 15 users cannot use lock screen widgets (iOS 16+ only)
**Impact**: Feature parity gap; may limit addressable market
**Mitigation**:
- Target iOS 16+ minimum (87% adoption as of May 2026)
- Fallback: Home screen widget for iOS 15 (if time permits)

### Unknown 1: Actual Task Completion Rate
**Unknown**: Do users actually complete tasks when forced, or just snooze indefinitely?
**Research Needed**: User testing with 5-10 power users post-MVP
**Impact**: If users bypass task forcing, entire value prop collapses

### Unknown 2: Snooze Behavior Patterns
**Unknown**: Do users snooze 1x, 3x, 10x? How does snooze pattern vary by user?
**Research Needed**: Analytics post-launch; instrument snooze count + task completion rate
**Impact**: Informs snooze duration defaults + UX design

### Unknown 3: Check-in Person Utilization
**Unknown**: Of users who enable accountability, what % actually use it?
**Research Needed**: User testing pre-MVP; analytics post-launch
**Impact**: If unused, feature adds complexity without value

### Unknown 4: Todoist / Third-Party Integration Demand
**Unknown**: What % of addressable market uses Todoist vs. Apple Reminders?
**Research Needed**: User survey post-MVP or pre-launch
**Impact**: If Todoist demand high, MVP limitation is significant blocker

---

## Summary

WakeTask solves a specific, validated gap: users with morning tasks who need forcing, not puzzles. By combining task forcing + optional accountability + calendar awareness + free pricing, WakeTask appeals to Alarmy users frustrated by bloat, SnoozeProof users seeking purpose, and I Can't Wake Up users who want privacy.

**MVP focuses ruthlessly on core value**: alarm + your task + completion forcing. Weeks 2-3 validate retention and account for habituation. Months 2-3 add calendar + accountability + third-party integrations.

**Addressable market**: 120-150K users actively frustrated with current solutions; potential growth to 20-50K reviews in 12-18 months based on competitor trajectories.

**Differentiation**: We're the first alarm app that prioritizes **your actual morning goal** over generic friction or gamification. That's a defensible, valuable position.

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_10","type":"agent_message","text":"# WakeTask Spec Candidate (GPT-5 Codex)

## 1. Problem Statement
People who snooze through alarms have only a 30–90 second attention window after waking. Existing alarm apps either allow easy dismiss (Clock) or force generic puzzles (Alarmy), but neither presents the user’s actual morning priority at the critical moment. Result: repeated snoozing, decision paralysis, and missed starts.

## 2. Target User
Primary:
- Adults 25–45 with flexible mornings (remote workers, students, knowledge workers)
- Already using iPhone + Apple Reminders
- Frequent snoozers (3+ snoozes/day) who want practical, not gamified wake-up forcing

Secondary:
- ADHD/neurodivergent users who need external structure
- Parents needing rapid morning prioritization
- Users who reject public/social accountability

## 3. MVP Feature Set
1. Alarm forcing core
- Single alarm configuration with recurrence (weekday/custom)
- Persistent alarm UI with critical alert behavior and strong haptics
- Only two actions at alarm: `Complete Task` or `Snooze`

2. Task-at-alarm integration
- Apple Reminders integration (read/write with permission)
- Pull top-priority morning task at trigger time
- Display task prominently on alarm screen (large type, high contrast)

3. Snooze-proof task re-presentation
- Snooze options: 10/15/30 min
- On every re-ring, task is shown again (not time-only)
- Completion marks task done in Reminders

4. Accessibility and reliability
- VoiceOver labels/actions for all controls
- Dynamic Type and high-contrast support
- Haptic-first cues for hearing-impaired users

5. Minimal settings
- Alarm time/recurrence, snooze duration, haptic intensity, reminder source
- No optional complexity in first-run path beyond permissions

## 4. Non-Goals (MVP)
- Games/puzzles/music wake challenges
- Public social posting/accountability feeds
- Financial stakes/payments
- Gamification/streak economy
- Multi-person/team accountability
- Calendar-aware dynamic wake time (post-MVP)
- Todoist/third-party task sources (post-MVP)

## 5. User Journeys
1. First-run setup
- User opens app, grants Notifications + Reminders
- Selects reminder list/source and alarm time
- Sees confirmation preview: next alarm + selected morning task

2. Morning alarm flow
- Alarm fires with task visible immediately
- User either snoozes or taps `Complete Task`
- If complete: task marked done, alarm ends, completion event logged
- If snooze: alarm re-triggers with same task context

3. Failure/recovery flow
- If Reminders permission revoked or no task found, app falls back to “Set one wake task now” quick entry
- Alarm still functions; user is blocked from silent dismissal loops

## 6. Acceptance Criteria
- Alarm screen always shows a concrete task when task source available
- User cannot fully dismiss ringing state without either task completion or explicit snooze
- Snooze always re-displays task content on next ring
- Task completion writes back to Reminders successfully (or surfaces actionable error)
- First-time setup to active alarm can be completed in under 2 minutes
- VoiceOver can navigate and activate all alarm actions
- Core app behavior validated on supported iOS versions without crashes in primary flows

## 7. Analytics / Events
Track on-device-first, privacy-minimized events:
- `onboarding_started`
- `permissions_notifications_granted`
- `permissions_reminders_granted`
- `alarm_created`
- `alarm_fired`
- `task_loaded_for_alarm`
- `task_missing_fallback_shown`
- `snooze_selected` (with duration)
- `task_completed_from_alarm`
- `alarm_dismissed_without_completion` (if allowed fallback path)
- `first_alarm_success_within_7_days`
- `daily_snooze_count`
- `time_to_completion_after_alarm`

Success KPIs:
- First/second-ring rise rate
- Avg snoozes per active day
- Task completion within 30 min of alarm
- Week-4 retention for active alarm users

## 8. Privacy / Security Requirements
- No hardcoded secrets or credential logging
- No env-dump commands or secret echoing in tooling/logs
- Reminders/calendar/health data processed on-device by default
- Data minimization: collect only event metadata needed for product metrics
- Explicit consent for each iOS permission; graceful degrade on denial
- Clear in-app privacy disclosure for what is stored locally vs optionally synced
- Security gates remain blocking: SwiftLint, SwiftFormat, Qlty, secret scanning

## 9. App Store 4.3 Differentiation Statement
WakeTask is not a clone alarm utility. Its core behavior is distinct: it binds alarm dismissal flow to the user’s own morning task (Apple Reminders), re-presents that task on every snooze cycle, and prioritizes purposeful wake-up execution over generic puzzles/games. The app is focused, privacy-first, and functionally differentiated from existing “challenge alarm” products.

## 10. Appium Exploratory Testing Requirements
- Implement exploratory tapper to click every reachable enabled control in key screens
- Required flows:
1. Onboarding and permission prompts
2. Alarm creation/edit
3. Alarm firing UI with complete/snooze paths
4. Settings changes (snooze duration, haptic intensity, task source)
5. Permission-denied fallback states
- Output artifacts:
1. Control coverage summary
2. Screens visited and action log
3. Any crash/hang with repro steps
- Must be part of quality gate evidence; cannot be skipped unless explicitly marked `allow_macos_deferred`

## 11. SwiftAIBoilerplatePro Module Reuse Plan
Reuse boilerplate platform modules; only extend app-specific domain:
- Reuse app shell/navigation/theme/settings scaffolding
- Reuse permission handling patterns and dependency injection container
- Reuse storage/networking abstractions where needed (without rebuilding infra)
- Add WakeTask domain modules:
1. `AlarmDomain` (alarm scheduling, snooze lifecycle)
2. `TaskBridge` (Reminders adapter, task selection, completion write-back)
3. `AlarmUI` (ringing state views/components)
4. `WakeAnalytics` (event contracts + emitters)
- Keep Swift 6 strict concurrency
- Keep production/test Swift files under 400 lines

## 12. Definition of Done
1. Product
- MVP flows implemented and match acceptance criteria
- Differentiation statement reflected in in-app copy and App Store metadata draft

2. Engineering quality
- Swift 6 strict concurrency clean
- SwiftLint/SwiftFormat/Qlty/secret scan all pass
- No file-size policy violations (>400 LOC per Swift file)

3. Validation
- `xcodebuild build` and `xcodebuild test` pass in macOS lane
- Appium exploratory tapper evidence attached with no blocker defects

4. Release readiness
- Privacy disclosures complete
- App Store 4.3 hardening checks pass for strings/metadata
- Spec, QA evidence, and handoff notes complete for TestFlight-ready state"}}
